FEMA Flood Map Service Center: Search By Address
================================================

Please enter a valid US address/place.x

Enter an address, place, or coordinates: [![Image 2: LOMC Help](https://msc.fema.gov/portal/images/icon-question-mark.png)](javascript:openHelpWindow('addresssearch');) 

Whether you are in a high risk zone or not, you may need [flood insurance](https://www.fema.gov/national-flood-insurance-program) because most homeowners insurance doesn't cover flood damage. If you live in an area with low or moderate flood risk, you are 5 times more likely to experience flood than a fire in your home over the next 30 years. For many, a National Flood Insurance Program's flood insurance policy could cost less than $400 per year. Call your insurance agent today and protect what you've built. 

Learn more about [steps you can take](https://www.fema.gov/what-mitigation) to reduce flood risk damage.

--- SOURCE 4: Flood Maps | FEMA.gov ---
URL: https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps

SUMMARY:
Flood Maps | FEMA.gov FEMA maintains and updates data through flood maps and risk assessments. The**FEMA Flood Map Service Center (MSC)**is the official online location to find all flood hazard mapping products created under the National Flood Insurance Program, including your community’s flood map. The mapping process helps you and your community understand your flood risk and make more informed decisions about how to reduce or manage your risk. Updates to flood maps are a collaboration between your community and FEMA. Every community that participates in the National Flood Insurance Program has a floodplain administrator who works with FEMA during the mapping process. Cooperating Technical Partners are communities, regional agencies, state agencies, universities and tribal nations that become more active participants in the FEMA flood hazard mapping program.

Flood Maps
==========

Floods are unpredictable. Because flood risk is always changing, communities use tools to understand their risk and take actions to protect their families, homes and businesses.

![Image 8: Illustration of a map in a flood](https://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2020-06/illustration_hero_floodmaps_0.png)

![Image 9: world globe](https://www.fema.gov/profiles/femad8_gov/themes/fema_uswds/images/world-icon-black.png)
*   [English](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps)
*   [Español](https://www.fema.gov/es/flood-maps)
*   [Kreyòl](https://www.fema.gov/ht/flood-maps)
*   [한국어](https://www.fema.gov/ko/flood-maps)
*   [Tiếng Việt](https://www.fema.gov/vi/flood-maps)
*   [简体中文](https://www.fema.gov/zh-hans/flood-maps)

Floods occur naturally and can happen almost anywhere. They may not even be near a body of water, although river and coastal flooding are two of the most common types. Heavy rains, poor drainage, and even nearby construction projects can put you at risk for flood damage.

Flood maps are one tool that communities use to know which areas have the highest risk of flooding. FEMA maintains and updates data through [flood maps](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/products-tools) and [risk assessments](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tools-resources/risk-map).

![Image 10: alert - info](https://www.fema.gov/profiles/femad8_gov/themes/fema_uswds/images/arrow.svg)
**Flood maps show how likely it is for an area to flood.** Any place with a 1% chance or higher chance of experiencing a flood each year is considered to have a high risk. Those areas have at least a one-in-four chance of flooding during a 30-year mortgage.

Floods don’t follow city limits or property lines. Using a flood map, you can see the relationship between your property and the areas with the highest risk of flooding. There is no such thing as a “no-risk zone,” but some areas have a lower or moderate risk.

How to View and Obtain Flood Maps
---------------------------------

The**FEMA Flood Map Service Center (MSC)**is the official online location to find all flood hazard mapping products created under the [National Flood Insurance Program](https://www.fema.gov/flood-insurance), including your community’s flood map.

[Map Service Center ![Image 11: External Link Arrow](https://www.fema.gov/profiles/femad8_gov/modules/custom/fema_gutenberg/modules/fema_link/images/icon_external.png)](https://msc.fema.gov/portal/home)[National Flood Hazard Layer](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tools-resources/flood-map-products/national-flood-hazard-layer)

### Using Flood Maps

Flood maps help mortgage lenders determine insurance requirements and help communities develop strategies for reducing their risk. The mapping process helps you and your community understand your flood risk and make more informed decisions about how to reduce or manage your risk.

![Image 12: community understand your flood risk and make more informed decisions about how to reduce or manage your risk graphic](https://www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/graphics/fema_flood-map-uses_graphic.png)

Change Your Flood Zone Designation
----------------------------------

Updates to flood maps are a collaboration between your community and FEMA. Every community that participates in the [National Flood Insurance Program](https://www.fema.gov/flood-insurance) has a floodplain administrator who works with FEMA during the mapping process.

FEMA provides the technology and relies on your community’s leaders to share local knowledge and plans to make the maps as accurate as possible.

*   FEMA and the floodplain administrator work with local engineers and surveyors to collect the data to inform the maps. 
*   Community members are also invited to provide information to help local officials better understand how water drains in the area. 
*   FEMA works with local experts before combining the data into a computer model that is used to create the updated flood map. 

### Learn More

About [Map Amendments (LOMA)](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/loma-lomr-f)&[Map Revisions (LOMR)](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/lomr-clomr)

[Process to Revise a Flood Map](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/revision-process)

[Paper Application Forms](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/paper-application-forms)

[Endangered Species Act Requirements](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/esa)

[Status of Map Change Requests](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/status)

[Letter of Final Determination](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/letter-final-determination)

Once the data analysis is done, preliminary flood maps will be available for review. Before your community decides to adopt the maps, you have 90 days to submit technical data to support an appeal to the map.

Any time after your community adopts the maps, you can submit data to amend or revise the flood map through the **Letter of Map Change (LOMC) process**.

[Submit a Letter of Map Change Request](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone)

[View the Community Members' Guide to Initiating Map Revisions](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/change-your-flood-zone/guide-community-members)

Products and Tools
------------------

### Regulatory Flood Map Products

Learn about [regulatory flood map products](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/products-tools) and access flood maps, Flood Insurance Studies, and FIRM databases.

### GIS and Other Products

[Manage data](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tools-resources/flood-map-products#gis) with Preliminary Flood Hazard Data, National Flood Hazard Layer, and Hazus.

### Risk Mapping, Assessment and Planning (Risk MAP)

These products provide a more user friendly analysis of flood risks within a[Risk MAP project](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tools-resources/risk-map).

### Tools to Help Use Products

*   [Tutorials](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tutorials)
*   [Software](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/tutorials/software)

Guidance and Partners
---------------------

### Guidelines & Standards

View Risk MAP [guidelines and standards](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/guidance-partners/guidelines-standards) for flood risk analysis and mapping activities

### Notices to Congress

FEMA provides a monthly [summary to Congress](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/guidance-partners/congress-notice) providing details on flood risk mapping activities.

### Advisory Council

Read about the [Technical Mapping Advisory Council](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/guidance-partners/technical-mapping-advisory-council), a federal advisory committee.

### Cooperating Technical Partners

[Cooperating Technical Partners](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/guidance-partners/cooperating-technical-partners) are communities, regional agencies, state agencies, universities and tribal nations that become more active participants in the FEMA flood hazard mapping program.

Manage Risk
-----------

### Levee Flood Risk

To help understand the [realities of levee risk](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/living-levees), FEMA provides community outreach tools and construction guidelines related to levee risk.

### Coastal Flood Risk

To help coastal communities understand and reduce their flood risk, FEMA has initiated [coastal flood risk studies](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/coastal) for 100 percent of the populated coastline.

### Know Your Risk

Tips for [homeowners, community officials, engineers, insurance professionals](https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/know-your-risk) and other stakeholder groups about understanding risk.

Contact Us
----------

For additional information or assistance, contact a Map Specialist in the[FEMA Mapping and Insurance eXchange (FMIX)](https://www.floodmaps.fema.gov/fhm/fmx_main.html).

[Sign up for emails](https://service.govdelivery.com/accounts/USDHSFEMA/subscriber/new?topic_id=USDHSFEMA_178)to receive flood hazard mapping updates.

# Hurricane Harvey

The U.S. Geological Survey has deployed storm-tide sensors, forecast what coastal change to expect, and is ready to measure the extent of flooding likely to result from this powerful storm, Hurricane Harvey.

### Event Support Map

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://i0.wp.com/californiawaterblog.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/harvey_map.png?fit=791,506&ssl=1
DESCRIPTION: The map displays the flood extent of Hurricane Harvey in Houston, highlighting areas affected by water during the storm, with flood zones shaded in shades of blue, brown, and orange.

IMAGE 2:
URL: http://www.weather.gov/images/hgx/projects/harvey17/5dayrainfalltotal_westgulfriverforecastcenter.PNG
DESCRIPTION: The map displays 5-day point rainfall amounts in inches from Hurricane Harvey, showing areas in Houston and surrounding regions affected by heavy rainfall, with color-coded zones indicating up to over 50 inches of rain.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.star.nesdis.noaa.gov/jpss/images/Harvey/show/VIIRS_375m_floodmap_Houston_Beaumount_20170831.png
DESCRIPTION: The map illustrates the automatic flood detection in the West Gulf region of the USA, displaying areas of varying floodwater fractions, with certain regions highlighted in red indicating significant flooding, particularly in coastal areas.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://printablemapaz.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/potential-storm-surge-flooding-map-texas-flood-zone-map-2016.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image shows a flood zone map of southeastern Texas indicating potential storm surge flooding from Hurricane Harvey, with areas color-coded based on the projected water height and including regions of leveed areas and intertidal zones.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://apps.urban.org/features/blog-graphics/2017/09/harvey-maps/images/FHA_flood.png
DESCRIPTION: The map displays flood zone risk levels from Hurricane Harvey in the Houston area, with varying percentages of FHA purchase loans in 2015 indicated by different shades of yellow and brown, alongside geographic labels of major cities and water bodies.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Hurricane Harvey caused unprecedented flooding in Houston, with rainfall exceeding 50 inches, affecting thousands of homes and displacing residents across multiple counties.

enriched_points.point_title: GIS Role in Mapping Flood Zones
enriched_points.expanded_content: GIS technology was crucial in mapping flood zones, enabling visualization of affected areas and aiding in risk assessments through detailed flood extent maps.
GIS facilitated coordination among various agencies in rescue operations, providing real-time data on flood zones and optimizing routes for emergency responders.
Utilizing GIS tools improved response times by allowing agencies to quickly identify high-risk areas and allocate resources efficiently during the disaster.
Key lessons included the need for updated flood maps, better inter-agency communication, and the importance of community preparedness in mitigating future disaster impacts.
